event_blocks.summary: Joshua Weissman, a man with long curly hair and glasses, introduced his ambitious plan to "make, taste, and rank the most popular dish in every single state in America," a concept visualized by a map of the United States and quick cuts of various regional foods, while humorously singling out Delaware for its lack of food culture. He explained that audience-voted dishes would be ranked on a tier list from A to F, with an "S-tier" for dishes competing in a "showdown." Before diving into the state dishes, Weissman excitedly announced the release of his cookbook, "joshua weissman: texture over taste," celebrating with golden confetti and by tossing the book in the air. Transitioning to the "DISH SHOWDOWN," Weissman first presented "Utah Funeral Potatoes," a cheesy potato casserole with cornflakes, which received a "C" from one taster and a "D" from Weissman, who noted the crunch as its saving grace. Next, the "New Jersey Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich," featuring "sliced pork product," American cheese, and a runny egg, was tasted by Weissman and two other men. Initial reactions were harsh, with one taster calling it an "F" and another noting it tasted like "preservative," but it ultimately landed a "C" tier. The segment then moved to "Ohio Cincinnati-Style Chili" over spaghetti, described as "Skyline can of chili over spaghetti with raw onion and shredded yellow cheddar." This dish elicited strong negative reactions, with one taster apologizing, another comparing its smell to "charcuterie pork," and the group ultimately assigning it a "D" and "F" tier.

He initially declares the Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich 'an F' but then reconsiders, stating 'The Taylor Ham's starting to grow on me. I'm still going to give it a C though.' Later, he reacts to the Cincinnati-Style Chili with a simple 'Nah' and assigns it a 'D.'

Cookbook titled 'joshua weissman: textur.description: A blue-covered cookbook with a photo of a woman on the front, titled 'joshua weissman: texture over taste', prominently displayed. Joshua Weissman describes it as 'the first cookbook of its time' focused on 'texture,' containing 'dozens of different recipes, techniques' including 'Nashville Hot Honey Karaage, brownies, greatest mac and cheese of your life.'

Funeral Potatoes.description: A creamy potato casserole topped with crispy cornflakes, shown being prepared in a white casserole dish and later served. Joshua Weissman describes it as 'a cheesy potato casserole' with 'cornflakes, tossed in butter, lightly seasoned, put on top of a creamy, cheesy, shredded potato mixture.' The Man in gray t-shirt rates it a 'C,' while Joshua Weissman rates it a 'D,' noting the 'crunch on top.'

Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich.description: A breakfast sandwich made with pork roll, egg, and cheese, shown being assembled and sliced, with a runny egg yolk oozing out. A voiceover guides its assembly. It's referred to as 'Taylor Ham' by the tasters, who debate its saltiness and overall appeal, ultimately receiving a 'C' from two of the men.

A voiceover describes it as 'Ohio Cincinnati-Style Chili,' specifically 'Skyline can of chili over spaghetti with raw onion and shredded yellow cheddar.' The tasters have strong, mostly negative reactions, with one comparing its smell to 'a white suburban mother's idea of charcuterie pork' and another, despite being from Ohio, 'fully disagree[ing]' with it, leading to 'F' and 'D' grades.

Dining table setting.description: A wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room with large windows, indoor plants, and a beige sofa in the background, serving as the primary setting for the food tasting segment.

Man in dark gray t-shirt.description: A man wearing a dark gray t-shirt, seated at the table, observing and reacting thoughtfully. He comments on the Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich, saying 'It doesn't taste like ham. It tastes like preservative.' Later, he expresses a strong personal connection to Ohio but 'fully disagree[s]' with the Cincinnati-Style Chili.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Joshua Weissman, a man with long curly hair, glasses, and a black apron, speaking directly to the camera in a modern kitchen. With an enthusiastic and humorous tone, he introduces his plan to "make, taste, and rank the most popular dish in every single state in America," as quick cuts display various regional dishes like pizza, gumbo, and roasted chicken, followed by a map of the United States. He continues, describing the USA as a "melting pot of food and culture," noting that "everyone seems to think that their state has the best food," but humorously adding that "some just straight up have no food culture," specifically calling out "Delaware." This is accompanied by a montage of social media comments suggesting iconic state foods like 'Skyline Chili' and 'Cali burrito'. Joshua then explains the show's premise, stating that the audience voted for dishes to represent their states, which will be ranked on "the most famous tier list on the internet," from A through F, with a special "S-tier" for "truly God tier" dishes that will compete in a "showdown." With a sudden shift in tone, he excitedly declares, "But first, I have the most important announcement of the year. My cookbook is officially out today!" while briefly seen on a couch reading a tablet before returning to the kitchen. A dramatic, upbeat electronic music track kicks in as a title sequence flashes, showing a pixelated dish with a question mark before revealing the bold yellow 'DISH SHOWDOWN' graphic against a fiery background. Joshua reappears in the kitchen, now surrounded by multiple copies of his blue-covered cookbook titled 'joshua weissman: texture over taste'. He places one on a wooden cutting board, then celebrates exuberantly as golden confetti rains down, tossing the book in the air and dancing joyfully to the music. The segment concludes with him in a gray t-shirt, sitting at a small table with the cookbook, smiling and gesturing as if presenting it.

The segment opens with an upbeat, celebratory track as Joshua Weissman, the Man with long curly hair and glasses, joyfully dances amidst golden confetti in his modern kitchen, proudly displaying his cookbook, 'joshua weissman: texture over taste'. He explains in his enthusiastic voice that he 'didn't want to make just another cookbook,' but rather 'the first cookbook of its time, focused on the one thing that no one ever talks about, which is texture,' featuring 'dozens of different recipes, techniques' like 'Nashville Hot Honey Karaage, brownies, greatest mac and cheese of your life.' After a close-up of the cookbook and a '75+ Recipes' graphic, he transitions to the video's content, introducing 'Utah Funeral Potatoes' as 'one of the strangest named dishes.' A graphic of Utah appears as he describes the dish as 'basically a cheesy potato casserole' with 'cornflakes, tossed in butter, lightly seasoned, put on top of a creamy, cheesy, shredded potato mixture,' baked for '1 hour.' He then sits with the Man in gray t-shirt, who rates the Funeral Potatoes a 'C,' stating 'it's not as bad as it looks,' while Joshua Weissman gives it a 'D,' adding it would be an 'F if it wasn't for the crunch on top,' concluding it's a 'D tier.' The scene shifts to a New Jersey graphic, where Joshua Weissman introduces the 'New Jersey Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich,' mentioning the 'big argument about whether or not this is Taylor Ham' before describing the cooking of 'sliced pork product' and the assembly of the sandwich, highlighting a runny egg yolk.

The segment opens with a close-up of hands expertly assembling a Pork Roll Breakfast Sandwich, with a voiceover guiding the process: adding American cheese, cooking an egg in pork fat, seasoning it, and placing a fried egg on top before closing it up. The voiceover notes the cross-section "doesn't look too bad" as the sandwich is sliced, revealing a gooey interior. The scene transitions to a bright, modern living room where three men sit at a wooden table, tasting the sandwich. The man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt with brown leather suspenders, initially declares, "That's an F," while chewing. The man in a gray striped hoodie reacts animatedly, exclaiming, "Oh my god, it really is that salty. Holy sh-" (bleeped). The man in a dark gray t-shirt adds, "It doesn't taste like ham. It tastes like preservative." The man in the gray striped hoodie then states, "I hate the Taylor Ham, but I love eggs, cheese and bread, so it's getting a C." The man with long curly hair and glasses reconsiders, saying, "The Taylor Ham's starting to grow on me. I'm still going to give it a C though." A graphic interface then highlights the New Jersey state graphic in yellow, as an energetic voiceover announces, "New Jersey lands at a C tier." The focus shifts to a bowl of Cincinnati-Style Chili over spaghetti, topped with shredded cheese, being served with a ladle. The voiceover introduces it as "Ohio Cincinnati-Style Chili," describing it as "Skyline can of chili over spaghetti with raw onion and shredded yellow cheddar." The men are shown tasting the chili with forks, displaying varied reactions. The man in the dark gray t-shirt, after a chew, apologetically says, "I uh, I'm sorry." The man in the gray striped hoodie gestures emphatically, stating, "To me, it smells like a white suburban mother's idea of charcuterie pork," and dramatically adds, "If an Italian saw this, I genuinely think that they would call the police immediately." The man in the dark gray t-shirt, despite being from Ohio, declares, "I was born in Ohio and I fully disagree with this." The man with long curly hair and glasses simply says, "Nah," while the man in the gray striped hoodie emphatically gives it "F, F." The man with long curly hair and glasses assigns it a "D." The man in the gray striped hoodie continues, "I think Ohio is like a beautiful state. If this ceased to exist, I would not care," as the group continues their discussion, with the chili bowl still on the table.

event_blocks.summary: The Man with long curly hair and glasses initiated the discussion by assigning an 'F' rating to pasta, a sentiment echoed by the Man in gray t-shirt who confirmed an 'F tier' for Ohio. The latter then introduced the 'Minnesota Tater Tot Hotdish,' described as 'Great Depression era' with beef, onions, vegetables, cheese, and tater tots, though the visual presented a creamy Funeral Potatoes casserole topped with crispy cornflakes, baked at 350° F. The Man in gray t-shirt found it 'kind of nice,' and the Man in dark gray t-shirt expressed enthusiasm for the tots, leading to ratings of 'C' from the Man with long curly hair, a 'solid B' from the Man in dark gray t-shirt, and an 'average C placement' from the Man in gray t-shirt. A graphic interface then highlighted New Jersey and Minnesota as the Man in gray t-shirt presented the 'Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger,' assembled on a griddle with a beef patty, melted cheese, and crispy fried onions. After tasting, the Man with long curly hair noted that smashing the onions elevated the dish, while the Man in dark gray t-shirt deemed it 'something special,' resulting in a unanimous 'S' tier rating, with the Man in gray t-shirt calling it 'Too classy.' The graphic interface reappeared, focusing on New Mexico, as the Man in gray t-shirt introduced the 'New Mexico Green Chili Cheese Burger,' which the group had voted for, concluding with a close-up of a sizzling patty being pressed on a griddle. Subsequently, Cam, in a gray striped hoodie, provided a voiceover detailing the assembly of a burger with green chilies and cheese, including mayo on the bun. The scene shifted to a dining table where Cam rated the burger an 'A,' the man in the dark gray t-shirt also gave it an 'A' but felt it could be improved, and the Man with long curly hair rated it a 'B,' finding it good but 'not a fun experience.' A graphic interface then displayed Wisconsin, highlighted as Cam introduced 'Wisconsin fried cheese curds,' which were presented in a bowl. The man in the dark gray t-shirt rated them a 'B,' comparing them to a 'small mozzarella stick,' while the Man with long curly hair declared them 'always an S in my book,' and Cam also gave them an 'S' rating. The video then transitioned to West Virginia, highlighted in white, with a close-up of a plate featuring a split bread roll and red sauce, as Cam introduced 'West Virginia's official food, the pepperoni roll,' before the segment concluded with dough being kneaded on a wooden board.

event_blocks.summary: Hands shape dough into a log and place it on a baking sheet before sliding it into a 400°F oven as a male voice narrates the process of making pepperoni rolls, admitting to guessing the recipe. The scene shifts to a bright dining room where a man with long curly hair and glasses, identified as Joshua, holds up his cookbook, 'joshua weissman: texture over taste,' promoting it with 'LINK IN DESCRIPTION.' Joshua then cuts the pepperoni roll, rating it a 'C,' while a man in a gray t-shirt gives it a 'B' and expresses willingness to eat it again, and another man in a dark gray t-shirt agrees but lacks enthusiasm. Joshua concludes the roll is 'C tier.' A North Carolina state graphic appears as Joshua introduces a pulled pork sandwich with 'Lexington style barbecue sauce,' described as 'a wee bit spicy' due to vinegar, tomatoes, and spices. A close-up shows the sandwich assembly with shredded meat, cheese, and sauce. The man in the gray t-shirt shares his 'long opinion about vinegary barbecue sauces' and rates the pulled pork a 'B,' a rating echoed by the man in the dark gray t-shirt, who states he 'probably wouldn't be looking for pulled pork sandwiches' even in North Carolina. Joshua takes a bite, chews thoughtfully, and gives a thumbs-up as the group continues their discussion.

event_blocks.summary: A man with long curly hair and glasses, seated at a dining table, initially rates North Carolina's dish as 'B tier,' a sentiment echoed by the man in the dark gray t-shirt. The scene shifts to a graphic interface of U.S. states and ratings, where the curly-haired man introduces 'Hawaiian Spam Musubi' as number 10, explaining his unique acceptance of Spam as a distinct entity rather than canned meat. He then demonstrates the musubi preparation, searing Spam slices, layering them with rice and a sweet soy glaze in a mold, wrapping it in nori, and cutting it. The focus then moves to Wyoming on the graphic interface, introducing 'Elk,' which the curly-haired man notes was the people of Wyoming's vote. He describes a seared elk chop, highlighting its nutritional benefits but low fat content, before seasoning it with salt and pepper, searing it, and basting it with herby garlic butter, which he melts over the chop with a torch, concluding the video with the glistening elk chop.

He declares North Carolina's dish a 'B tier' generally, which the man in the dark gray t-shirt agrees with, before the curly-haired man concludes, 'So that came in at a B tier.' The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states and ratings, as the curly-haired man announces, 'Number 10, Hawaiian Spam Musubi.' He explains his unique acceptance of Spam, stating, 'It is quite literally the only canned meat I accept because it's not canned meat. It's its own thing.' Visually, Spam slices are seared in a cast-iron skillet and then assembled into a musubi with rice and nori using a white plastic mold, as he narrates the process: 'You're gonna need a musubi mold... layer in some rice, some sweet soy glaze Spam... press it down, wrap that puppy up and split in half.' After cutting the musubi, he exclaims, 'I mean, it's beautiful, brother. I love...' and takes a bite with a satisfied expression, prompting an 'Oh!' from the man in the gray striped hoodie. He praises it as 'a perfect food,' while the man in the dark gray t-shirt notes its 'nice cross-section.' Despite finding it 'really hard,' he rates it 'a solid A tier,' with the man in the gray striped hoodie adding 'A plus plus' and 'So close to S.' He muses it would be an 'S if we were from Hawaii,' ultimately giving it 'an A,' concluding it's 'Coming in at a ripe A tier.' The scene then shifts to Wyoming on the graphic interface, and he introduces 'Elk,' acknowledging, 'If you're confused, so am I. This is what the people of Wyoming voted for.' A beautifully seared elk chop with a pat of butter is shown on a white plate as he describes it as 'a Wyoming elk steak, which is apparently pretty good for you. High in protein, zinc, B12, but contains a chef's worst nightmare: low fat.' He then lifts the chop to reveal its juicy interior before placing it back and using a torch to melt the butter, creating a golden glaze, while narrating, 'Seasoned it lightly, salt and pepper, seared it, and basted it with a nice herby garlic...' The video concludes with the glistening elk chop under the torch flame.

event_blocks.summary: At a modern dining table, the Man in dark gray t-shirt describes the food as "pretty gamey" but enjoyable, while the Man with long curly hair and glasses rates it a "D," stating he doesn't "really eat game." The Man in dark gray t-shirt explains his "F" rating by comparing it to other foods, noting it "just didn't taste that special," and the Man in gray t-shirt adds "F for no fat." The scene shifts to a graphic interface of U.S. states, where the Man in dark gray t-shirt selects 'Texas' and humorously warns against voting for "Waterburger," revealing that "brisket" received the majority of votes. A close-up shows an elk chop being seared and butter melted with a torch, prompting the Man with long curly hair and glasses to discuss the "enjoyment of food" and how "good brisket never fails." The video then shows a large cut of beef being seasoned, smoked, and sliced to reveal a pink interior. Returning to the dining table, the Man with long curly hair and glasses and the Man in gray t-shirt both place brisket in the "A-tier," with the Man in dark gray t-shirt suggesting it's "an A-tier" for Texans and "an S-tier" for out-of-towners. The graphic interface highlights 'New Mexico' as the Man in dark gray t-shirt concludes they are giving brisket a "solid, highly, highly rated" score, before ending with a close-up of a seared salmon fillet.

It is visually presented while the discussion shifts to brisket, implying it was the 'gamey' food being discussed earlier.

He contributes to the food discussion by suggesting the 'F' rating is 'for no fat' and agrees with the 'A-tier' rating for brisket.

He expresses his liking for 'gamey' food but rates it an 'F' in comparison to other dishes, clarifies the 'F' rating, humorously warns against 'Waterburger' for Texas, and ultimately rates brisket as 'A-tier' for locals and 'S-tier' for out-of-towners, concluding with a 'highly, highly rated' assessment.

raw_segments: The video opens with a lively scene at a wooden dining table in a bright, modern living room, where three men are engaged in animated conversation. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, gesturing expressively with his silver watch and ring visible, comments on the food being "pretty gamey" but that he likes it. The Man with long curly hair and glasses, listening intently, then interjects, stating he wants to give it a "D," elaborating that he doesn't "really eat game" and while he appreciates it, "for me it's enough." The Man in dark gray t-shirt adds his perspective, stating he likes "wild game" and it's "great," but to represent all the food they'll eat today, he rates it an "F." The Man in gray t-shirt, who had been listening thoughtfully, chimes in with "F for no fat." The Man in dark gray t-shirt clarifies that an "F" rating doesn't mean it's "bad," but rather a comparison to other foods, concluding it "just didn't taste that special." The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with a vertical rating scale. A cursor selects 'Texas' in red, as the Man in dark gray t-shirt announces, "So let's F this up with number 12, Texas. This is where we live." He humorously warns against voting for "Waterburger" to represent Texas, threatening to "ship you out of here, brother," before revealing that the majority of votes for Texas went towards "brisket." A close-up of a perfectly seared elk chop on a white plate, topped with a pat of butter melting under a torch, is shown. The Man with long curly hair and glasses speaks about the "enjoyment of food," specifically "things melting in your mouth," and how "good brisket never fails to do that." The video cuts to a kitchen setting where a large cut of beef is seasoned, smoked, and then sliced to reveal a juicy, pink interior. The scene returns to the dining table, where the Man with long curly hair and glasses states he'd put it in the "A-tier," a sentiment echoed by the Man in gray t-shirt ("I'm going A as well"). The Man in dark gray t-shirt elaborates, suggesting it's "an A-tier" for Texans who eat smoked brisket regularly, but "an S-tier" for out-of-towners. The Man in dark gray t-shirt makes expressive hand gestures, and the Man with long curly hair and glasses nods. The graphic interface reappears, highlighting 'New Mexico' in yellow, as the Man in dark gray t-shirt concludes that "for us, we're giving brisket a solid, highly, highly rated..." The video ends with a close-up of a seared salmon fillet on a white plate, glistening with seasoning and a golden crust.

event_blocks.summary: Two salmon fillets grill over an open flame on a wooden plank, with a male voice announcing "A tier" and introducing "Number 13, Washington Cedar Plank Salmon." The scene transitions to a seared salmon fillet on a white plate, and the speaker expresses uncertainty before tasting. In a bright living room, three men are seated at a dining table for a food tasting, with a graphic interface showing U.S. states and ratings, highlighting Texas in red. Upon tasting the salmon, the Man with long curly hair and glasses exclaims, "That's so good!" praising its "good smokiness" and awarding it an "A," a sentiment echoed by the Man in dark gray t-shirt. The Man in gray t-shirt notes it's "really close" to smoked salmon but "not exactly the same," yet "an impressive thing to eat." The Man with long curly hair and glasses emphatically concludes, "We got to give it an A for me. A shocking A tier." The scene then shifts to biscuits being drizzled with chocolate gravy, with the text 'Chocolate Gravy' appearing. The Man with long curly hair and glasses introduces "Number 14, Arkansas Biscuits with Chocolate Gravy," initially calling it "probably one of the worst things I've ever seen." The Man in dark gray t-shirt suggests it "might be the most American thing we have," while the Man in gray t-shirt questions if it's dessert. After tasting, the Man with long curly hair and glasses exclaims, "This is amazing! You can't beat that," and the Man in dark gray t-shirt agrees, "Wow, that works out way too well." The Man in gray t-shirt admits he "wanted to hate this a lot." The Man with long curly hair and glasses enthusiastically declares, "Arkansas, holy sh*t!" giving a thumbs-up and concluding it's "a well-deserved S for me," as the text 'LINK IN DESCRIPTION' appears on screen.

event_blocks.summary: Three men sat at a dining table, debating rankings as a graphic interface displayed U.S. states and ratings; the man in the dark gray t-shirt questioned why something wasn't 'S tier' while another suggested it was, and the man with long curly hair and glasses interjected. The man in the dark gray t-shirt expressed frustration with a simple dish, ultimately giving it an 'A+++' and noting Arkansas made it to 'S tier' with a 'Yeehaw!' The scene shifted to a graphic interface where Texas was highlighted, and a narrator announced 'Number 15, Missouri. Toasted Ravioli,' showing golden-brown ravioli with marinara and cheese, described as a 'St. Louis favorite' that's 'golden brown delicious.' Back at the table, the men tasted the ravioli, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt rating it 'C,' the man in the gray striped hoodie a 'B,' and the man with long curly hair and glasses a 'D,' leading to an average of 'C tier.' The graphic reappeared, highlighting Nevada, and the narrator introduced 'Number 16, Nevada,' revealing 'shrimp cocktail' as the top-voted item, which was then shown being dipped in red sauce. The man in the dark gray t-shirt critiqued it as 'really boring from a culinary standpoint' and a 'D,' while the man in the gray striped hoodie summarized their ratings as 'a D, a B, and an A.' The segment then showed a dark-themed graphic interface with a male voice questioning 'it out to what? A C?' as the narrator announced 'Number 17, Delaware,' highlighting the state and transitioning to 'Habersett Scrapple,' described as loved by Delaware despite being from Pennsylvania. The scrapple was sliced and pan-fried, and at the table, the man in the dark gray t-shirt exclaimed 'Smelly Deli. Oh, God, I don't want to eat this,' calling it 'F tier,' while the man with long curly hair and glasses tasted it thoughtfully. The narrator introduced 'Number 18, Rhode Island, Calamari,' despite a South Dakota graphic, showing fried calamari with peppers and onions, described as 'little fried calamari, tossed in a little garlic butter, some hot peppers.' The man in the gray striped hoodie found it tasted like 'anything fried,' the man with long curly hair clapped in approval, the man in the dark gray t-shirt called it 'one of your best takes ever' but gave it a 'strong powerful C,' the man in the gray striped hoodie gave an 'A,' and the man with long curly hair rated it a 'strong powerful B.' The narrator then announced 'Number 19, South Dakota, Chislic,' with a South Dakota graphic, followed by grilled meat skewers, described as seasoned and deep-fried. The men discussed it at the table; the man in the dark gray t-shirt found it 'not bad,' the man in the gray t-shirt called it 'pretty good,' the man with long curly hair rated it an 'A,' the man in the dark gray t-shirt gave a 'solid B,' and the man in the gray t-shirt also gave a 'B,' averaging out to a 'B.' The scene transitioned to a graphic interface where Texas was selected, and a creamy Knoephla Soup was shown, introduced by the man with long curly hair and glasses as 'Number 20, North Dakota,' who struggled with the pronunciation and described it as a 'creamy German chicken and dumpling soup' with a 'good feeling about this one.' At the table, the man in the dark gray t-shirt exclaimed 'God damn,' and the man in the gray t-shirt said 'Very good.' The man with long curly hair and glasses rated the soup an 'A,' calling it 'creamy, salty, rich,' 'surprisingly balanced,' and highlighting the 'chewy dumplings,' an 'A that aspires to be an S,' which the man in the dark gray t-shirt agreed to keep at 'A tier,' confirmed by an Alabama graphic. The segment shifted to 'Number 21, Alabama barbecue chicken with white barbecue sauce,' with the man with long curly hair and glasses expressing surprise about white barbecue sauce, as creamy sauce was poured over grilled chicken.

raw_segments: The segment opens with three men seated at a wooden dining table in a bright living room, engaged in a lively discussion. The man in the dark gray t-shirt exclaims "Oh, what!" and questions why a previous item isn't "S tier," while the man in the gray striped hoodie suggests "I'll go S tier for this," and the man with long curly hair and glasses interjects "Whoa, whoa, whoa." The man in the dark gray t-shirt then passionately states, "I hate that this is so simple and dumb. I don't want to give it an S. I'm going to give it an A+++... almost S because part of me just doesn't feel right about this, but it doesn't matter. Somehow Arkansas wrassled its way into S tier," concluding with a "Yeehaw!" The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states, where a cursor selects Texas, highlighted in red, as a narrator announces, "Moving on to number 15, Missouri. Toasted Ravioli." A close-up reveals golden-brown toasted ravioli with marinara sauce, garnished with grated cheese, with text overlays 'Toasted Ravioli' and 'Golden Brown Delicious,' while the narrator describes it as a "St. Louis favorite" that's "breaded and fried 'til good old GBD, that's golden brown delicious." The men return to the table to taste the ravioli; the man in the dark gray t-shirt takes a bite and reacts with delight, then rates it "C." The man in the gray striped hoodie gives it a "B," adding, "I kind of liked it," while the man with long curly hair and glasses rates it "D," explaining, "I just don't need it to be like that." The man in the dark gray t-shirt summarizes, "So we'll average it out at C tier." The graphic interface reappears, highlighting Nevada in white, as the narrator introduces "Number 16, Nevada," and explains the difficulty in finding a dish for the desert state, revealing the "number one voted item was shrimp cocktail." A close-up shows cooked shrimp being dipped into red sauce. The men resume their discussion, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt gesturing enthusiastically as he critiques, "I wouldn't put it all the way up in an A. This is really boring from a culinary standpoint. This is just a shrimp cocktail. I think it's a D." The man in the gray striped hoodie then summarizes the group's ratings, "So we got a D, a B, and an A," as these letters appear sequentially on screen, before the man with long curly hair and glasses begins to speak, with his words cutting off.

The segment opens with a dark-themed graphic interface displaying U.S. states and a rating scale, as a male voice questions, "it out to what? A C?" A narrator then announces, "Number 17, Delaware," highlighting the state. The scene transitions to a package of 'Habersett Scrapple,' which the narrator explains is from Pennsylvania but loved by Delaware. The scrapple is sliced, pan-fried to a golden-brown crust, and served. Three men are seated at a wooden dining table in a bright living room. The man in the dark gray t-shirt exclaims, "Smelly Deli. Oh, God, I don't want to eat this. Gosh," and later vividly describes it as feeling like "throw up" before declaring it "F tier." The man with long curly hair and glasses takes a bite with a thoughtful expression. The narrator then introduces, "Number 18, Rhode Island, Calamari," despite a graphic of South Dakota appearing on screen. Fried calamari with peppers and onions is served, and the narrator describes it as "little fried calamari, tossed in a little garlic butter, some hot peppers." The man in the gray striped hoodie states, "I don't like calamari, but it kind of just tastes like anything fried." The man with long curly hair claps in approval after tasting it. The man in the dark gray t-shirt calls it "one of your best takes ever" but gives it a "strong powerful C." The man in the gray striped hoodie gives an "A" rating, mentioning he grew up in the Northeast, while the man with long curly hair rates it a "strong powerful B." The narrator then announces, "Number 19, South Dakota, Chislic," as a graphic of South Dakota reappears, followed by grilled meat skewers being prepared and served. The narrator questions saying the word 'Chislic' out loud and notes, "It's seasoned..." The men continue their discussion, with the man in the dark gray t-shirt gesturing expressively and the man in the gray striped hoodie listening thoughtfully.

The video opens with a close-up of hands threading raw beef cubes onto a wooden skewer, which is then seared in a red Dutch oven, transforming into golden-brown Chislic. A male voice explains that this dish, often lamb, mutton, or venison, is seasoned and deep-fried. The scene transitions to a bright, modern dining table where three men are seated, engaging in animated discussion. The Man in dark gray t-shirt finds the Chislic "not bad," while the Man in gray t-shirt calls it "pretty good." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, gesturing expressively, rates it an "A," but the Man in dark gray t-shirt gives it a "solid B," noting there's "nothing wrong with it." The Man in gray t-shirt also gives a "B," suggesting a higher rating if a traditional accompaniment were present. The Man with long curly hair and glasses concludes their discussion, stating, "So we average out at a B." The scene transitions to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where a cursor selects Texas. A close-up of a creamy, yellow Knoephla Soup garnished with herbs and black pepper appears, with the text 'Knoephla Soup'. The Man with long curly hair and glasses introduces it as "Number 20, North Dakota," struggling to pronounce "Knoephla" ("Nofla, Knofla, Nofle"), admitting he's "not great with pronunciations." He describes it as a "creamy German chicken and dumpling soup," expressing a "good feeling about this one." The men return to the table, where the Man in dark gray t-shirt exclaims "God damn," and the Man in gray t-shirt says "Very good." The Man with long curly hair and glasses rates the Knoephla Soup an "A," elaborating that "it's creamy, it's salty, it's rich," and "surprisingly balanced," highlighting the "chewy dumplings" as its unique feature. He calls it "an A that aspires to be an S," to which the Man in dark gray t-shirt agrees to "keep it at an A tier." An 'Alabama' state graphic then confirms the rating. The segment shifts to a new dish: White BBQ Sauce, introduced by the Man with long curly hair and glasses as "Number 21, Alabama barbecue chicken with white barbecue sauce." He expresses surprise, having "always thought white barbecue sauce was illegal" due to his mom being from Texas, as the thick, creamy sauce is generously poured over grilled chicken pieces on a white plate, with the text 'White BBQ Sauce' displayed.

event_blocks.summary: The Man in dark gray t-shirt described grilled chicken pieces drizzled with White BBQ Sauce as "nice smoked chicken" with a "mayo vinegar-based white barbecue sauce" that might prevent drying. At a dining table, the Man in gray t-shirt rated the chicken an "A," while the Man with long curly hair and glasses gave it a "B minus," calling it "close to a C." The Man in dark gray t-shirt assessed it as "good" but "B tier," stating he "wouldn't care" if he never had it again. A Virginia state graphic preceded the introduction of "Country Ham" as "Number 22," with the Man in dark gray t-shirt explaining they put it "on a sandwich" served on a buttered biscuit. The Man with long curly hair and glasses gave it a "D," to which the Man in dark gray t-shirt responded negatively, saying he would eat it "if I had to." The Man with long curly hair and glasses expressed shock at the "D tier" rating, but the Man in gray t-shirt was not surprised. A graphic interface showed state ratings before transitioning to Iowa for "Number 23," the "Iowa Chop." The Man in dark gray t-shirt identified it as a "pork porterhouse" and questioned Iowa's claim. He detailed searing the pork chops in a cast-iron skillet with herbs and butter, basting them with "butter, fresh thyme, rosemary, bay leaf," and finishing with a brown sauce, concluding, "It's gonna be good, I mean, but it's just a pork chop." The final shot showed the plated Iowa Chop with pronunciation variations displayed as the Man in dark gray t-shirt warned about saying it correctly before tasting.

On-screen rating letters.description: Large, colorful letters 'A' and 'D' appearing on screen, likely representing the men's ratings or responses to the dishes, with 'A' in yellow and 'D' in green. The 'B' and 'D' ratings are explicitly stated by the men.

It is described as 'just a pork porterhouse' and its cooking process involves searing, flipping, and basting with 'butter, fresh thyme, rosemary, bay leaf'.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of golden-brown grilled chicken pieces being generously drizzled with a creamy white sauce, identified as 'White BBQ Sauce'. The Man in dark gray t-shirt describes it as a "nice smoked chicken" with a "mayo vinegar-based white barbecue sauce," speculating it keeps the chicken from drying out. The scene transitions to a bright, modern dining table where three men are seated. The Man in gray t-shirt, gesturing, declares the chicken "actually really good" and rates it an "A." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, with expressive hand movements, gives it a "B minus," noting it's "close to a C." The Man in dark gray t-shirt offers a lukewarm assessment, stating it's "good" but he "wouldn't care" if he never had it again, as an on-screen 'B' rating appears, confirming his "B tier" assessment. Next, a graphic of the state of Virginia appears as the Man in dark gray t-shirt introduces "Number 22, Virginia," explaining that "everybody voted for Country Ham." Hands are then shown assembling a biscuit sandwich, slicing a golden-brown biscuit and placing country ham inside, while the Man in dark gray t-shirt explains they decided to "give Virginia a helping hand" by putting the ham "on a sandwich," noting it's traditionally "served as a sandwich on a buttered biscuit." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, now with a green 'D' rating graphic overlaid on his chest, simply states "D." The Man in dark gray t-shirt responds, "I don't like that," elaborating that he would eat it "if I had to" but "only if I had to eat it." The Man with long curly hair and glasses exclaims, "Oh my god, I'm shocked, a D tier?", while the Man in gray t-shirt calmly replies, "I'm not shocked." The scene shifts to a graphic interface displaying U.S. states with color-coded ratings, where the cursor highlights Virginia, then transitions to Iowa as the Man in dark gray t-shirt introduces "Number 23, Iowa," identifying the dish as "the Iowa Chop." A plate of seared pork chops, labeled 'Iowa Chop', is shown as the Man in dark gray t-shirt describes it as "just a pork porterhouse," questioning why Iowa claims it. A cooking sequence follows, showing the chops seared in a cast-iron skillet with herbs and butter, as he details the process of searing, flipping, and basting with "butter, fresh thyme, rosemary, bay leaf." The chops are then finished with a rich brown sauce poured over them, and he concludes, "It's gonna be good, I mean, but it's just a pork chop." The final shot shows the plated Iowa Chop with on-screen text displaying various pronunciations ('Iowans', 'Iowahs', 'Iowoahs') as the Man in dark gray t-shirt warns about the pronunciation before tasting.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a close-up assembly of a Pork Tenderloin Sandwich, highlighting its Iowa origin with a graphic, as a male voice introduces the segment and transitions to a living room setting where three men are seated at a dining table. The man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and suspenders, rates the pork chop a "C," a sentiment echoed by the man in the dark gray t-shirt and the man in the gray striped hoodie, who collectively deem it "C-tier." The scene shifts to a preparation of Smoked Salmon Spread, featuring fresh herbs and red sauce, served on toasted bread with an Alaska graphic. During this, the man with long curly hair describes the Indiana Pork Tenderloin Sandwich in detail, from its "bottom bun, yellow mustard, massive... comedically large piece of fried pork" to "raw onion, mustard on top bun, lid it and eat it," confirming its authenticity. The man in the dark gray t-shirt praises it as a "fun eating experience" allowing for varied bites, affirmed by the man in the gray striped hoodie and the man in the dark gray t-shirt as "a good sandwich," with the man with curly hair ultimately rating the best version a "B." As the Alaska Smoked Salmon Spread is presented, the man with long curly hair provides a detailed recipe: "mix cream cheese and salmon, add some sliced chives, brunoise shallots, season with Tabasco, Worcestershire, and lemon juice, a little salt, packed it into a ramekin beautifully." The dining table and living room backdrop remain consistent, fostering a warm atmosphere for the food discussion.

event_blocks.summary: The Man with long curly hair and glasses, the Man in dark gray t-shirt, and the Man in gray t-shirt initially rated fried chicken an 'A.' The scene shifted to a black screen with a Louisiana graphic, introducing 'Number 31, Louisiana: Gumbo,' featuring a steaming pot of Gumbo stirred on a stovetop. The Narrator/Host described his controversial 'land and sea' Gumbo recipe, which he defended by referencing Cajun practices, before ladling it over white rice and garnishing it with green onions, proclaiming it 'the daddy of all soups and stews.' Back at the dining table, the Man with long curly hair and glasses tasted the Gumbo, considering an 'A' but seeing potential for an 'S,' while the Man in dark gray t-shirt found it 'very good' and 'well-balanced,' and the Man in gray t-shirt awarded it an 'S.' The Man with long curly hair and glasses then upgraded his rating to an 'S,' calling it 'one of the most influential things that exist in food today.' The video then shows a person assembling a burrito on a wooden cutting board, with the man in the center narrating the 'shockingly simple' process: guacamole is spread on a tortilla, followed by sour cream, caramelized onions, shredded Monterey Jack cheese, and cooked carne asada, with a mention of 'crispy french fries' as an ingredient. He instructs to roll it tightly, then reveals the 'secret' to searing it seam-side down in a black cast-iron skillet on a gas stove on medium heat to 'lock that burrito shut,' accompanied by a sizzling sound. After searing, the burrito is removed, sliced in half, and presented on a white plate with two small ramekins of sauce. The three men return to the table, where the man in the center announces, 'Let's taste.' The man in the dark gray t-shirt holds a piece of the burrito, praises it as having 'all the best elements,' and gives an 'A' rating, which appears as a large golden 'A' on screen. The man in the center then declares he's going to give an 'S' rating, agreeing with the previous assessment and highlighting the 'crunch from the french fries,' as a large red 'S' appears on screen. The atmosphere is casual and engaging, with the men actively participating in the food tasting and rating process.

event_blocks.summary: Josh, a man with long curly hair and glasses, enthusiastically described a California burrito as "creamy, cheesy, beefy," and "perfectly balanced," rating it an "8.5" while Vikram, the man in a dark gray t-shirt, scored it a "9" and Cam, the man in a gray t-shirt, gave it a "2," leading to a total score of 19.5. The group then tasted "Arkansas biscuits with chocolate gravy," with Vikram scoring it a "5" and both Josh and Cam a "1." Next, the "Smoked Salmon Spread" from Alaska received scores of "one" from Vikram, "five" from Josh, and "two" from Cam, totaling eight points. The "Pork Chile Verde Stew" earned a "7" from Vikram and a "three" from Cam, with Josh questioning the high score. Maryland Crab Cakes were introduced, with Vikram scoring "7," Josh "6" (later clarified as "2"), and Cam "8," resulting in a total of 21 points and a new lead. New England Clam Chowder was tasted, with Vikram and Josh both scoring "2," and Cam a "4," despite Josh calling it "one of my most favorite soups of all time" but not "that special." Louisiana Gumbo received a "7" from Josh, a "9" from Vikram, and a "6" from Cam, totaling 22 points. "Lemon Pepper Wet Wings" were scored "8" by Cam and "6.5" by Vikram, with Josh noting "there's nothing special about wings." The "Italian Beef Sandwich" received perfect "10" scores from both Josh and Vikram. The "shredded meat sandwich" was rated "7" by Cam and "9" by Vicram, with the "Italian beef" sandwich receiving a "6" from the man in the gray t-shirt, leading to Illinois scoring 24 points. The "Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger" was awarded a "9" by Vicram and a "9" by Josh, and a "7" by Cam, making it the winner with 25 points.

raw_segments: The video opens with Josh, the Man with long curly hair and glasses, seated at a dining table, gesturing as he enthusiastically describes a burrito as "creamy, cheesy, beefy," and "perfectly balanced," confidently declaring the "California burrito S tier." The scene widens to show him flanked by Vikram, the Man in dark gray t-shirt, and Cam, the Man in gray t-shirt, all seated at a wooden table in a bright, modern living room. A serious voiceover, accompanied by dramatic background music, then introduces an S-tier ranking system, visually displayed on a dark-themed graphic interface with U.S. states, where California is highlighted. The voiceover announces the first item: the California Burrito. Josh is seen eating a piece of the burrito while the others watch. The men then give their scores: Vikram rates it a "9," Josh an "8.5," and Cam a "2," who exclaims, "What the f-! There's better burritos out there!" Vikram calmly disagrees, while Josh, perhaps playing devil's advocate, states, "Just because there's fries on it, doesn't mean it's top tier." Vikram explains his high score, noting the burrito's combination of flavors and admitting his "extremely biased" Californian origin. A close-up of the burrito, cut in half to reveal its filling, is shown as the voiceover announces the "California Total Score: 19.5." The video transitions to a plate of Chocolate Gravy poured over golden-brown biscuits. The voiceover introduces "Arkansas biscuits with chocolate gravy," and the men give their next scores: Vikram a "5," and both Josh and Cam a "1," with Cam expressing incredulity. Josh is seen eating a spoonful of the chocolate gravy, followed by a shot of him holding up a card with '1' written on it, with the text 'JOSH 1.0' displayed. The video concludes with Josh holding an 'S' card and Cam holding an '8' card.

The video segment opens in a bright, modern living room where three men, Josh, Cam, and Vicram, are seated at a wooden dining table for a food tasting competition. Josh, in a black t-shirt and suspenders, initially holds a '1.0' card, while Cam, in a gray striped hoodie, holds a '2.0' card, and Vicram, in a dark gray t-shirt, holds a '5.0' card. The group discusses the 'Arkansas' score, with Josh commenting on it being "the biggest accomplishment for Arkansas this year," followed by laughter. Cam expresses his positive surprise, saying he "liked it so much more than I thought I was going to be," while Josh humorously adds he's "still upset that it exists." Vicram offers a nuanced positive review, finding a dish "very inventive" and praising its "combination." On-screen graphics show Arkansas with a total score of 7 points, which Josh confirms as "strong 7 points." The focus then shifts to the 'Smoked Salmon Spread,' introduced with an Alaska state graphic and close-ups of the dish. Josh announces the scores for the salmon dip: Vicram's score is announced as 'one,' though he is later seen revealing a '7.0' card for a different dish. Josh's own score is 'five,' matching the '5.0' card he later reveals. Cam's score is 'two,' matching the '2.0' card he displays, prompting Cam to exclaim, "What the f***, guys." Josh then gives a sarcastic review, stating, "It tastes incredible. There's not one ounce of anything special about this." Vicram, however, praises the salmon dip for its "essence and enjoyment," while Cam expresses a preference for "other smoked fish dips." Josh summarizes Alaska's score as "eight points." The next dish, 'Pork Chile Verde Stew,' is introduced with a close-up, and Josh reacts to Vicram's '7.0' score with "Jesus, a seven?" He then announces Cam's score as 'three,' despite Cam being visually seen holding a '2.0' card, and sets the stakes, noting they "need a 9.5 or higher to take the lead." Vicram enthusiastically describes the chili verde as "such a good way for green chili to come through," adding he'd "actually go to the state and ask for that," a sentiment Josh immediately contradicts, stating, "I would not go to the state and ask for it." The segment concludes with a ranking graphic showing California in first place with 19.5, Alaska with 8, and Arkansas with 7, as the men continue their lively and competitive discussion.

The segment opens with a close-up of Josh, the Man with long curly hair and glasses, speaking animatedly about 'pork belly stew,' rating it a 'three' and explaining that while it was 'amazing,' he could think of 'three different Mexican dishes' he'd 'much rather have.' A narrator's voice then announces 'Green Chili Pork, 13 points.' The scene transitions to Cam, the Man in gray t-shirt, holding up a white card with a large '7,' followed by digital overlays showing 'CAM 3.0' and then 'CAM 8.0' as the narrator announces 'Maryland Crab Cakes. Vikram 7, Me 6, Cam 8.' The camera pulls back to reveal all three men at the wooden dining table: Cam on the right holding a gray card, Josh in the center gesturing, and Vikram, the Man in dark gray t-shirt, on the left, clasping his hands. A scoreboard graphic appears, listing California (19.5), Colorado (13), Alaska (8), and Arkansas (7). A close-up of a white card with a large '8' is shown before returning to the three men, with Cam now holding a gray card displaying '8.0.' Cam defends his score, stating crab cakes are 'the best way to eat crab. To me.' Josh, with 'JOSH 6.0' then 'JOSH 2.0' overlays, speaks with expressive hand gestures, calling crab cakes 'special' and mentioning he's 'from that area of the country,' seeking them out as a child, and not seeing them 'too many other places.' Vikram, with a 'VICRAM 2.0' overlay, thoughtfully resting his chin on his hand, interjects 'I think there are six,' followed by laughter. Josh then clarifies, 'I love them. But if I was given all sorts of choices... I just wouldn't grab them immediately.' The narrator announces 'Total 21 and our new leader is Maryland Crab Cakes,' as the scoreboard updates to show Maryland at the top with 21 points. The video cuts to a close-up of a bowl of New England Clam Chowder, garnished with bacon bits, herbs, and butter, with 'Clam Chowder' text overlaid. The scene returns to the three men, with Josh holding a white card with a '2' and a 'JOSH 2.0' overlay, and Vikram, with a 'VICRAM 2.0' overlay, looking engaged. The narrator announces the scores for clam chowder: 'Vikram 2, Me 2, Cam, well, 4, whoa, okay.' Josh explains his low score despite it being 'one of my most favorite soups of all time,' because 'there are at least four or five soups I can think of that are almost identical in flavor or borderline the same. It's not that special of a soup.' Cam then reveals, 'Funny enough he says that, today was my first time having clam chowder, but it tasted like I've had it before. That's why I'm...' The bright, modern living room with large windows, indoor plants, and a beige sofa provides a consistent, inviting backdrop throughout the food tasting.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Man in gray t-shirt, identified as Cam, smiling and gesturing with his hands as he states, "I'm going to get into it too." The Man in dark gray t-shirt, Josh, then shares his enthusiasm, declaring, "I just love clam chowder." The scene transitions to a wide shot of the three men—Josh, the Man with long curly hair and glasses (Vikram), and Cam—seated at the dining table, each holding a score card. A narrator introduces "Louisiana Gumbo." Josh reveals a card with '7', displayed as 'JOSH 7.0' on screen. Vikram then reveals a card with '9', shown as 'VICRAM 9.0', gesturing excitedly and explaining, "You can't really get a bad bite of gumbo, and that's why I ranked it so high." Cam follows, revealing a card with '6', displayed as 'CAM 6.0', and speaks with a thoughtful expression about the dish's versatility, stating, "It's very based off of so many different cultures that you can go around the world and find something similar to gumbo." Josh expresses his seasonal preference, adding, "I don't want to eat gumbo when it's 115 degrees outside," to which Vikram counters, "I didn't see it as a seasonal thing. All year round, I'd enjoy it." A ranking graphic appears, showing Louisiana at #1 with a total score of 22. The next dish, 'Lemon Pepper Wet Wings', is introduced by the narrator with a close-up of the glossy, peppered wings. Cam reveals a card with '8', shown as 'CAM 8.0', praising them as "definitely one of the top ones for sure." Vikram reveals a card with '6.5', explaining, "I agree with that, but that's why I gave it a 6.5," while Josh offers a nuanced opinion, saying, "There's something special about this wing, but there's nothing special about wings." The ranking graphic updates, with Georgia now at #1 with 23 points. The final dish, 'Italian Beef Sandwich', is introduced by the narrator with a close-up of the sandwich being held. Josh reveals a card with '10', indicating a perfect score and remarking, "That's what I thought." The scene ends with Vikram also holding a card with '10', as the narrator begins to announce Cam's score.

The segment opens in a bright, modern living room where three men are seated at a wooden dining table, holding score cards. The Man in dark gray t-shirt, identified as Cam, reveals a '7' for the hearty shredded meat sandwich, stating that it "does not need and should never have cheese" and is "good on its own." The Man with long curly hair and glasses, Vicram, then reveals a '9', praising the sandwich as inventive, tangy, sweet, and "the top sandwich for me." The Man in gray t-shirt shows a '6', commenting that "an Italian beef is one of the most well-balanced sandwiches you can eat." On-screen graphics confirm their scores: 'CAM 7.0' and 'Vicram 9.0', alongside a 'Vicram 10' graphic. The scene cuts to a close-up of the shredded meat sandwich being assembled, followed by a ranking graphic displaying U.S. states and their scores for the sandwiches, with a voice announcing "Illinois 24 points" as a "new leader." The focus then shifts to the "Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger," with a voice setting up the next round. A close-up shows the burger, featuring a beef patty, melted cheese, and creamy sauce, being assembled on a wooden cutting board. Vicram is shown again, now with a '9.0' score for the burger, and a voice declares, "Vicram, a nine. Mine, a nine." The voice adds that "we need a seven or higher to win this" and notes that "Cam has not been the nicest." Cam then scores a '7' for the burger, leading to the announcement, "We have a winner, the Oklahoma Fried Onion Burger with 25 points." The voice expresses, "I crave this item by itself." The Man in gray t-shirt offers his opinion on the burger, calling it "an amazing burger, but did it reinvent the wheel? No." Vicram enthusiastically praises the burger, stating, "Listen, it's America. There's nothing better than a burger. This is definitely one of the top ways to make a burger. You just can't beat melted cheese, grilled onions, and a smash patty." The final shot reveals the completed burger with a bold 'WINNER' graphic and the Oklahoma state flag, as a voice concludes, "The burger might not have been created in America, but the burger was owned by America."

event_blocks.summary: A man with long curly hair and glasses, wearing a black t-shirt and brown leather suspenders, sits at a dining table in a bright, modern living room, addressing the camera with enthusiasm about the prevalence of great food across the United States and encouraging viewers to support local restaurants. He thanks those who submitted their favorite state foods and promotes his blue-covered cookbook, 'joshua weissman: texture over taste,' highlighting its focus on texture and its availability via a link in the description. A close-up shot then reveals hands slicing a piece of smoked beef on a cutting board, emphasizing its texture. Returning to the man, the text 'LINK IN DESCRIPTION' appears on screen as he concludes his monologue by asking viewers to like and subscribe if they enjoyed the video. The scene transitions to include two other men, one in a dark gray t-shirt and another in a gray striped hoodie, seated at the table. All three engage in a food tasting: the man in the dark gray t-shirt eats, the man in the gray hoodie observes, and the man with long curly hair holds a plate with a half-eaten pork roll breakfast sandwich and a knife. Upbeat, modern, slightly funky instrumental music plays throughout this segment, fostering a casual, conversational atmosphere as they react to and discuss the food.
